title: Team building 2026: Playing games, seriously
excerpt: One week ago, the KLR team stepped away from everyday roles and routines to reconnect simply as people. Through play, reflection and shared experiences, we explored what makes a growing, international and multidisciplinary team truly work.
contentHtml:
The Human Side of Corporate Culture

One week ago, the KLR team stepped away from everyday roles and routines to reconnect simply as people. Through play, reflection and shared experiences, we explored what makes a growing, international and multidisciplinary team truly work.

Beyond Roles and Routines

One week ago, our team stepped away from the usual routines to focus on something essential to every company: being together, free from daily roles, processes and responsibilities.

In the age of AI and technology, what still makes us stand out is the subtle art of being human - not simply performing functions.

In our imperfect diversity, we continue to transcend any algorithm. Exploring those “glitches in the matrix” and understanding the group dynamics behind them is an important task in every human-centred working environment.

Playing to explore

There is one thing humans learn to do before almost anything else: play.

I would even say that playing is what allows us to learn many other things later in life. As an NLP Master, I strongly believe that games are one of the best ways to explore how we approach the serious game called life—including its side quests: work, relationships, routines and responsibilities.

So that is what we did. We played.

The first part of the day was dedicated to gamified group experiences designed to explore how we play the game of business.

We observed our team dynamics, communication styles and approaches to solving challenges together. We also uncovered some of the hidden rules we follow simply because, at some point, we decided those rules existed.

The experience showed us where freedom can feel like a burden rather than an opportunity—and how limitations can sometimes become a cheat code for achieving results.

It also brought together colleagues who do not usually interact in their daily work. We gained valuable insights into how we can improve our communication, while confirming something equally important: we are a strong team made up of highly creative minds.

 

From Reflection to Summer Fun

The second part of the day was dedicated to pure summer fun.

We sailed along the Slovenian coast, where one of KLR’s headquarters is located. It was a chance to connect informally, strengthen relationships and enjoy being together as one team - playing, dancing, swimming and simply relaxing.

The Human Side of Corporate Culture

At KLR, we believe team building is much more than a formal social activity. It is an investment in trust, communication, shared understanding… and loyalty to what we do.

This is particularly important in an international, multicultural and multidisciplinary company like ours. As we grow across markets, bring together different perspectives and work across multiple processes, strong and respectful relationships help us collaborate more effectively and transform human diversity into a real business advantage.

Great teams are not built only through discipline, project management and technology adoption alone. They are built through shared experiences, honest communication, the willingness to understand one another better, commitment to shared goals and values beyond numbers - and the deeply human ability to have fun.

And we do.

title: KLR Is Ready to Move Beyond Europe
excerpt:
After ten years of building loyalty expertise across European retail, KLR is ready to take its next step beyond Europe.
For us, international growth is not about exporting a formula. It is about adapting proven experience to the realities of each local market, with curiosity, par
contentHtml:
Overview

After ten years of building loyalty expertise across European retail, KLR is ready to take its next step beyond Europe.

For us, international growth is not about exporting a formula. It is about adapting proven experience to the realities of each local market, with curiosity, partnership and long-term value at the centre.

The belief that shaped KLR from the beginning remains the same: loyalty is not a campaign, but a strategic capability that creates lasting value for retailers, brands and customers alike.

Ten years ago, KLR was built around a simple idea: loyalty should create lasting value, not just short-term sales.

Working alongside retailers across Europe, we learned that successful loyalty programs are never built around discounts alone. They are built around customer behaviour, trust and relevance.

Today, after a decade of experience, we are asking ourselves a different question: not "How do we grow further in Europe?"

But "How can a decade of experience become relevant in markets beyond Europe?"

For us, expanding beyond Europe is not growth for its own sake. It is the natural evolution of a company that has spent the past 10 years building expertise that can create value far beyond its home market.

More Than Campaigns

Over the past ten years, KLR has built far more than a portfolio of successful campaigns.

We have developed a methodology grounded in experience. Working across grocery and fuel retail has taught us that while every market has its own dynamics, the principles of loyalty remain remarkably consistent. Customers want to feel recognised. They value relevance. And they reward brands they trust.

Those insights are not limited by geography. They are the foundation we bring to every new partnership.

Retail Is Changing Everywhere

Retail is evolving at an unprecedented pace.

Customers expect seamless experiences across physical and digital channels, while retailers are under increasing pressure to create sustainable growth rather than short-lived promotional spikes.

These are not European challenges. They are global ones.

Our belief is simple: the answer is never to export a formula, but to adapt proven expertise to the realities of each local market.

Our Next Chapter

Our ambition is not to become a European company operating abroad.

Our ambition is to become an international loyalty partner.

That means listening before proposing, adapting before replicating and combining proven expertise with genuine curiosity about every market we enter.

International growth is never a one-way journey. Every new partnership teaches us something, making us stronger not only abroad, but also back home in Europe.

At KLR, we have always believed that loyalty is not a campaign. It is a strategic capability that creates long-term value for retailers, brands and customers alike.

That belief has not changed in ten years.

The future of loyalty will not be built by exporting ideas. It will be built by adapting them, market by market, partner by partner.

That's the journey KLR is starting today.

title: Unlock the Power of Loyalty for Your Brand
excerpt: For global and premium brands, retail loyalty campaigns offer a powerful way to reach millions of consumers in a trusted, high-frequency shopping environment. As KLR attended Licensing Expo Las Vegas 2026, Stefano Finazzi explores how licensing and loyalty can work together to tr
contentHtml:
Overview

For global and premium brands, retail loyalty campaigns offer a powerful way to reach millions of consumers in a trusted, high-frequency shopping environment. As KLR attends Licensing Expo Las Vegas 2026, Stefano Finazzi explores how licensing and loyalty can work together to transform brand equity into meaningful consumer engagement. Through close collaboration with brand owners, KLR designs each collection around the brand’s identity, strategy and goals — turning licensed products into high-impact loyalty experiences that drive market expansion, visibility and long-lasting consumer connection.

How KLR empowers global brands through licensing and retail loyalty

Licensing in KLR has always been about extending the value of a brand. It allows a name, a story, a design language or a lifestyle to move beyond its original category and enter people’s everyday lives.

But today, the strongest brand extensions are not only those that reach consumers. They are the ones consumers actively choose, collect, earn and keep.

Licensing Expo Las Vegas 2026 is the ideal place to explore how brands can move beyond traditional extensions and create deeper consumer connections. At KLR, we collaborate closely with brand owners to design each collection around their identity, positioning and objectives — transforming licensed products into loyalty experiences that are strategic, coherent and commercially effective.

With occasion of visiting this event, I want to talk more about where retail loyalty becomes a strategic growth opportunity for brands.

For global and premium brands, loyalty campaigns offer access to millions of consumers in a trusted, high-frequency shopping environment. While digital advertising competes for seconds of attention, a well-designed loyalty reward can live in a customer’s home for years. A pan used every evening, a backpack carried to school, a knife set displayed in the kitchen — these are not fleeting impressions. They are daily brand experiences.

At KLR, we help brands unlock this potential with high-impact loyalty campaigns across Europe. We connect brand value with retail power, turning licensing into a scalable, measurable and emotionally engaging growth channel.

From visibility to experience

Retail loyalty campaigns are not traditional product promotions. They create a relationship between the consumer, the retailer and the brand.

When customers collect stamps, reach a threshold or redeem a reward, the product becomes more than something they just buy. It becomes something they achieve. The brand is not simply purchased; it is earned. This creates a stronger emotional association and positions the product as aspirational, desirable and valuable.

For premium and global brands, this distinction matters. In a loyalty campaign, the brand becomes the centrepiece of a story, supported by in-store communication, digital engagement, POS materials and the credibility of a trusted retail chain.

A faster route to International markets

One of the biggest challenges for brands expanding internationally is the complexity of distribution. New markets require local knowledge, retail relationships, logistics, compliance, packaging adaptation and marketing execution.

KLR helps reduce this complexity.

Through our network of more than 150 retail clients across over 20 European markets, we help licensed brands enter new countries without the cost and operational burden of building direct distribution from scratch.

  • For brands, this means rapid market expansion. 
  • For retailers, it means access to desirable collections that drive engagement and strengthen customer loyalty.
  • For consumers, it means the opportunity to bring home premium products through a rewarding shopping journey.

High-volume exposure, long-lasting impact

KLR has delivered more than 340 loyalty campaigns supported by omnichannel visibility across entire retail chains.

This creates exposure that many brands would struggle to achieve through traditional channels alone. A loyalty campaign places the brand in front of millions of shoppers repeatedly — in store, on retailer apps, through leaflets, digital channels, CRM, social media and point-of-sale displays… And a loyalty reward remains present in everyday life. It becomes useful, visible and familiar. Over time, this daily presence builds recognition, affinity and trust. The consumer does not just remember the brand; they interact with it.

That is the unique power of loyalty.

From licensing to in-store: a seamless process

For many brand owners, the opportunity is clear, but execution can be complex. That is why KLR offers a complete, seamless process designed to protect the brand while maximizing campaign impact. From licensing and product strategy to collection development, multi-market deployment and retail integration, we manage every step of the journey. 

Our teams identify the right product lines, adapt them to the loyalty format, create campaign-ready collections and coordinate logistics, fulfilment, POS materials, digital visibility and in-store execution across countries… And your brand becomes more visible, desirable and well-known.

Licensing gives brands the right to travel.
Loyalty gives them the environment to matter.
Together, they create a powerful growth model that combines emotional brand value, retail trust, high-volume reach and measurable results.

Why licensing and loyalty belong together

 At KLR, we help brands move from licensed products to lived experiences — creating campaigns that deliver market access, consumer engagement and long-term brand presence. As the global licensing industry gathers in Las Vegas for Licensing Expo 2026, this is the opportunity we bring to brands: transforming campaign exposure into lasting impact and unlocking the power of loyalty in retail.

Discover Brands Who Work With Us!

title: Brands Are the New Loyalty Currency: Field Notes from Licensing Expo 2026
excerpt: Licensing Expo 2026 made one thing clear: brands are becoming a new form of loyalty currency. As consumers, especially Gen Z and Gen Alpha, look for cultural connection, self-expression, and authentic fandom, loyalty programs can no longer rely on points, discounts, or generic re
contentHtml:
Overview

Licensing Expo 2026 made one thing clear: brands are becoming a new form of loyalty currency. As consumers, especially Gen Z and Gen Alpha, look for cultural connection, self-expression, and authentic fandom, loyalty programs can no longer rely on points, discounts, or generic rewards alone. From entertainment and F&B to sports, fashion, and heritage IP, the strongest opportunities now sit at the intersection of recognition, emotion, and identity. For retailers, fuel networks, and grocery brands, licensed rewards offer a powerful way to turn everyday transactions into moments customers genuinely want to engage with, collect, and share.

Licensing Expo 2026 closed at Mandalay Bay on May 21 with the largest exhibitor floor on record: 410 exhibitors, 5,000+ brands, 12,500+ attendees, 1,500+ qualified retailers and a 28% international audience from 78 countries. Licensing International put the industry at $389.8B+, growing 5.45% YoY. License Global's own Top Global Licensing Agents whitepaper had fashion (80%) as the most strategically important category and food & beverage (68%) as the category to watch into 2027.
Meanwhile in loyalty land: ~90% of online adults already belong to a program, declared brand loyalty keeps falling, and most programs are loyalty in name only. Licensing is growing because consumers — especially the youngest ones — are starving for cultural material to attach themselves to. That gap is the opportunity.

1. Fandom > frequency

Netflix CMO Marian Lee opened the show. Founder Reed Hastings' doctrine, repeated on stage: “We don't start with revenue. We start with the fan.”
The proof points:

  • Stranger Things final season: Jazwares, Chips Ahoy!, Target, Primark — final episode released in theaters.
  • KPop Demon Hunters: an industry-first Hasbro + Mattel co-master toy partnership, plus Spirit Halloween, Loungefly, and Korean skincare brand Anua. Netflix shopped the IP for nearly two years pre-launch; partners hesitated, then scrambled.
  • Bridgerton × Dove “Let Them Talk” skincare collection ahead of Season 4.
  • ONE PIECE × Quiksilver apparel.
  • Netflix House — permanent immersive locations in King of Prussia (2025), Dallas (early 2026), Vegas Strip 2027. Free entry, restaurant + store + photo moments, ticketed VR/arcade/mini-golf, city-specific merch that sells out.

Loyalty translation: two members with identical receipts can have wildly different lifetime value — one feels nothing, the other tells friends about a collectible. 

The next-gen KPI isn't basket size. It's whether the program earned the right to be part of someone's identity.

2. Gen Zalpha is the demographic loyalty must design for

This is the single most important update for anyone planning 2027–2028 loyalty calendars. License Global dedicated an entire report to it.

The size of the prize

  • 40% of global fashion spending within a decade (Boston Consulting Group), spending 7% more of disposable income on clothing/shoes than prior generations.
  • Gen Z = 40% of the global consumer market today (Edelman).
  • Childrenswear = $171B globally and projected to outperform wider apparel by 2028 (Euromonitor) — driven by Asia Pacific, MEA and Latin America.
  • 8 of 10 of Gen Alpha's favourite brands overlap with Gen Z's. They move as a single tribe.
  • Top agent priorities: Gen Z (30%), Gen Alpha (17%) — the two biggest demographic targets in the 2026 Top Agents whitepaper.

How they actually buy

  • ~60% of Gen Alpha discovers brands online; 63% of all shoppers are more likely to buy from a trusted influencer (Traackr).
  • ~50% of Gen Z has bought a dupe (Mars United Commerce). Top dupe categories: cosmetics, fragrances, luxury. High-recognition / high-quality brands are immune.
  • Gen Z spends $2,000+/yr on beauty (Statista). 6–12-year-olds spent $4.7B on makeup and skincare in 2023 — more than any other generation (aytm).
  • Numerator (Sept 2025) on Gen Alpha apparel decisions: comfort 47% universal; for 11–14-year-olds → fit 46%, style 35%, brands 26%; for 1–5 → color/design 42%, price 23%.
  • They live inside Roblox, Fortnite, Web3 worlds (Pudgy Penguins, Labubu). Pop Mart's Labubu IP alone earned $419M in 2024, contributing to the company's $1.81B revenue.
  • Gen Z nostalgia bias: 42% listen to music to escape, 37% feel nostalgic for the '90s (GWI) — even though most weren't alive in it. Vibe over experience.
 

What this means for loyalty marketing design?

The default loyalty rewards mechanic — points → discount → generic premium — fails this audience on every axis. They don't experience a logo umbrella as a gift; they experience it as a tell.

What works instead:

  • Identity-first reward design. Every licensed item has to read as a self-expression object, not a freebie. Pop Mart, Labubu and chaotic customization (WGSN) — keychains, pins, patches, charms — are the dominant aesthetic.
  • Co-creation over distribution. e.l.f.'s Glow Up! on Roblox, Pudgy Penguins' character co-creation model. Programs that let members do something with the IP outperform programs that just give it to them.
  • Speed and drops over seasons. Short cycles, smaller drops, faster turnaround — the licensing model is moving away from annual collections. Loyalty calendars need to follow.
  • Mission and values as a filter. Edelman: Gen Z trusts brands more than any other institution — and punishes inauthenticity faster than any prior generation. The selection criterion is no longer “is this IP hot,” it's “does this IP have the right to be in this customer's life.”
  • The Eastern and Central European angle is live. Mr. SCRUBBER × The Smurfs in Ukraine, LPP/Sinsay × Miraculous in Poland, Sesame Street activating at Milano 2026 — these are signals that the Zalpha collab playbook is already operating in our markets, not waiting for it.

3. F&B and alcohol licensing is now a category, not a novelty

68% of agents call F&B the category to watch into 2027. Day 2 keynote: MARS Snacking, Pacsun, Bath & Body Works, CAA Brand Management on the same stage.

Recent collabs worth knowing by name:

  • Tabasco × ABSOLUT (spicy vodka) — riding the spicy trend: 66% of consumers like or love the term.
  • Pringles × Crocs, Haribo Goldbear centenary suite (toys, apparel, footwear, homeware via IMG).
  • Liquid Death × e.l.f. Cosmetics, Guinness × Van Leeuwen Ice Cream, Guinness × Higgidy, Sunday Swagger, Finsbury, Farmer.
  • Pepsi Prebiotic launched digital-first with Gen Z creators, then rolled to retail for the Super Bowl.
  • Girl Scouts Thin Mints × Wendy's Frosty, Coca-Cola × Star Wars, Friends Central Perk Coffeehouse (NYC, Nov 2025).
 

For grocery and fuel retail loyalty: F&B is the lowest-friction licensed reward category that exists. The shopper already has a daily relationship with the brand. You're not asking them to learn something — you're letting them materialize something they already love. Make it a default, not a curiosity.

4. Sports licensing has become lifestyle infrastructure

First dedicated Sports Pavilion. World Cup ahead, F1 surging, post-Olympics momentum. Brands in the room: Real Madrid, FC Barcelona, Chelsea, Newcastle, NASCAR, MotoGP, F1, MLB, NFLPA, ATP, Panini America. Categories now expanding into lifestyle, home, gaming, wellness, experiential.

The Eastern and Central European window is now. A football-anchored reward collection buys two years of associative memory at the cost of one campaign cycle.

5. Authenticity and cultural IP are eating opportunism

Two parallel signals from the floor:

  • Cultural IP is outperforming: the Van Gogh Museum's licensing program — BE@RBRICK via MEDICOM TOY (selling out in days since 2019), Casely tech accessories, BAPE 2025 — is the proof case that heritage IP with narrative depth beats trend-chasing entertainment IP for premium audiences. 
  • SEGA posted $1.3B in licensed consumer product revenue in 2025, up 58% from $950.8M, by leaning into character depth (Shadow, Knuckles, Amy) and underserved fans rather than chasing one hit.
 

For loyalty: every licensed reward needs two filters, not one — will it sell AND does it have the right to be in this customer's hands? Get the second wrong and the program loses trust faster than it earns redemptions.

49% of attendees with global decision power were retailers: Walmart, Target, Kohl's, Primark, Miniso, Hot Topic, Crocs, Tonies, Dick's Sporting Goods, Pop Mart, TJX, Bath & Body Works, Adidas, Zara.

6. The real audience was the retailer

Retailers go to Vegas to assemble next year's emotional architecture — which collaborations they'll back, which fandoms they'll service, which IP becomes the spine of their seasonal calendar. Loyalty calendars do the same job. Agencies that serve these retailers need to operate at the same altitude.

Seven shifts to plan around

  1. Portfolio over campaigns. 12–18 month IP mix: one nostalgic anchor, one fandom moment, one F&B comfort license, one sports tentpole, one Zalpha collab.
  2. Design every program with the Zalpha filter on. Even if today's program members skew older, the customers entering your stores in 2027–2028 won't.
  3. F&B as default for grocery and fuel. Zero learning curve, maximum recognition.
  4. Lock in sports IP now. World Cup and F1 windows close fast.
  5. Authenticity filter on every selection. Written test before production. Heritage and cultural IP increasingly out-earn trend IP at the premium end.
  6. Layer experiential on physical. Netflix House is the canary — a collectible plus an unlockable beats either alone.
  7. Loyalty = first-party data engine for licensed IP. Post-cookies, it's the cleanest place to learn which fandoms your customers actually belong to — and which Zalpha micro-trend is forming inside your basket data before it surfaces on TikTok.

The future of loyalty lies beyond points and discounts but in programs that earn a place in customers’ identities.

The most important sentence from Vegas wasn't said on stage. It was implied by the room: people — and especially the youngest ones — are starving for cultural material to attach themselves to, and loyalty marketing is one of the few channels that can deliver it as something the customer actually wanted, not as an ad.

Programs built around that fact will pull away. The rest will keep optimizing point ratios.

title: Promotion Awards: Special Mention in the Licensing Category for Oracle Red Bull Racing Adrenaline Campaign for Kaufland Bulgaria
excerpt:
Proud to share that our Oracle Red Bull Racing – Adrenaline campaign for Kaufland Bulgaria received a Special Mention in the Licensing category at the Promotion Awards 2025/26 – 20th edition.
A recognition that celebrates the power of combining strong licensing, retail expertise,
contentHtml:
Overview

Proud to share that our Oracle Red Bull Racing – Adrenaline campaign for Kaufland Bulgaria received a Special Mention in the Licensing category at the Promotion Awards 2025/26 – 20th edition.

A recognition that celebrates the power of combining strong licensing, retail expertise, and emotional loyalty experiences.

Thank you to Kaufland Bulgaria, Oracle Red Bull Racing, Red Bull, and KLR team involved.

We are proud to share that our Oracle Red Bull Racing – Adrenaline campaign for Kaufland Bulgaria has received a Special Mention in the Licensing category at the Promotion Awards 2025/26 – 20th edition.

This recognition is especially meaningful as Promotion Awards celebrates its 20th edition, confirming its role as the key reference point in Italy for professionals working in relationship marketing, loyalty programmes, incentives, and customer engagement.

Promoted and organised by Promotion magazine, the awards — formerly known as the Loyalty Awards — highlight the best practices in the sector, recognising campaigns and platforms selected according to objective criteria and evaluated with the contribution of a qualified jury.

The award ceremony took place on 13 May at Officine del Volo in Milan, Italy. The evening brought together more than 200 managers, representatives of the loyalty business in Italy: agencies, creatives, retailers and industry professionals to celebrate the most successful campaigns and platforms of the year.

Our Oracle Red Bull Racing – Adrenaline campaign for Kaufland Bulgaria was designed to turn everyday shopping into an engaging, high-energy loyalty experience. By combining the strength of a world-renowned licensed brand with Kaufland’s retail reach and KLR’s expertise in tailor-made loyalty solutions, the campaign connected customers with emotion, exclusivity, and memorable rewards thanks to impressive in-store hype.

For KLR, this Special Mention is more than just an award. It is a recognition of what we believe loyalty marketing should be: strategic, human-centred, emotionally relevant, and capable of creating real value for brands, retailers, and their customers.

A sincere thank you to Kaufland Bulgaria, Oracle Red Bull Racing, Red Bull, and to the KLR teamwork behind this exceptional campaign and shared ambition behind this whole project.

title: A Decade with KLR: Growing Together
excerpt: Our Head of Logistics, Nina Bjelivuk, celebrates 10 years with KLR by sharing her personal experience of growing alongside the company. From the early days of a two-person team to becoming part of a thriving international business, Nina reflects on the challenges, achievements, l
contentHtml:
Overview

Our Head of Logistics, Nina Bjelivuk, celebrates 10 years with KLR by sharing her personal experience of growing alongside the company. From the early days of a two-person team to becoming part of a thriving international business, Nina reflects on the challenges, achievements, loyalty, and human connections that have made her journey with KLR so meaningful.

Celebrating 10 years with KLR is a very special and emotional milestone for me. When I joined the company, we were only two people, starting to build something new step by step. There was a lot of learning, a lot of effort, and also a lot of belief that something great could grow from those early days. Today, seeing that we have grown into a team of so many people makes me incredibly proud, because I have had the privilege to experience this journey almost from the very beginning.

More Than a Workplace

Over the years, KLR has become much more than just a workplace for me. It has been a place where I was able to grow, learn, and face many challenges together with an amazing team. There were moments of hard work, but also many achievements that we celebrated together. What always stood out to me was the spirit of collaboration and the trust between people. I have always felt that we were building something together, not just doing a job.

The Meaning of Our Work

Working in loyalty campaigns has always had a special meaning for me. Our projects help large retail chains connect with their customers in a more engaging way, turning everyday shopping into a rewarding experience. When customers collect stickers and finally redeem them for products they are excited about, it creates a small moment of happiness. Knowing that our work contributes to those experiences and helps retailers build stronger relationships with their customers makes me truly passionate about what we do.

The People Behind the Journey

What makes these ten years even more meaningful are the people. KLR has always been a place where I felt supported not only professionally, but also personally. During different moments in my life, I always knew I had understanding, encouragement, and people around me who genuinely cared. That kind of support is something very rare, and it is one of the reasons why my connection to this company is so strong.

Living Loyalty Every Day

For me, loyalty is not only something we create for our clients through campaigns — it is also something we live every day inside the company. Loyalty grows through trust, shared experiences, and the feeling that you are part of something meaningful. These past ten years represent exactly that for me.

Looking Ahead

I am truly grateful for everything we have built together so far, and I feel excited about the future. If the first ten years have taught me anything, it is that great things can happen when people believe in the same vision and support each other along the way. I look forward to continuing this journey with the same passion, commitment, and loyalty that have defined these past ten years.

title: The Role of Data in Loyalty Programs
excerpt:
Sticker-based loyalty campaigns may feel nostalgic, but at KLR-Evropa, we power them with smart data and automation.
By tracking collection behavior and linking to digital tools, we unlock real customer insights—who’s collecting, what’s driving engagement, and where to optimize.
contentHtml:
Overview

Why Data and Automation Are Crucial for Smarter, More Effective Sticker-Based Campaigns?

At KLR, we specialise in a classic and beloved form of customer engagement: sticker-based loyalty campaigns. Customers collect stickers through purchases and redeem them for high-quality physical products at discounted prices. These campaigns are simple, tangible, and powerful in building footfall and boosting basket size.

But while the mechanic may be traditional, the intelligence behind it is anything but.

 

Sticker Campaigns: Where Tradition Meets Data

Sticker-based product collections have been a retail staple for decades. However, the real power of these programs today lies in what happens behind the scenes: the data collection, customer insight, and smart automation that elevate campaigns from promotional to strategic.

Here’s why data and automation now play a central role—even in physical loyalty campaigns:

1. Understanding Customer Behaviour Beyond the Sticker

Collecting stickers tells you more than just who’s participating. When paired with digital tracking—via loyalty cards, apps, or even linked POS data—you can learn:

  • Who your most loyal customers are
  • What products drive sticker collection
  • How campaign performance varies by region, store type, or customer profile

 

These insights allow you to fine-tune future campaigns—adjusting sticker thresholds, product choices, or communication strategies to fit real customer behaviour.

2. Optimising Campaign Timing and Communication

With automation, you no longer need to rely on static calendars and manual email blasts. Instead:

  • Trigger reminders when a customer is close to completing their sticker card
  • Notify customers when stock of the reward item is running low
  • Send personalized messages based on collection history (e.g., "You're just 3 stickers away!")

 

This real-time, behaviour-driven communication improves redemption rates and drives higher engagement.

3. Smarter Inventory and Supply Planning

By analysing how quickly stickers are collected and which rewards are most requested, you can:

  • Predict demand across different store locations
  • Avoid over- or under-stocking of high-demand items
  • Tailor assortments in future campaigns based on actual interest

 

This makes your supply chain more efficient and your promotions more profitable.

4. Measuring What Works—And Improving What Doesn’t

Sticker campaigns shouldn’t end with the last redemption. Using data analytics, you can track:

  • How much each campaign lifts average basket size
  • Whether it brings back lapsed customers
  • Which customer segments are most engaged

This creates a feedback loop that helps evolve your campaign design over time—from fixed schedules to data-optimised loyalty experiences.

Final Thoughts: Elevating Loyalty, Not Just Rewarding It

Physical product collections are emotional. They’re tangible. Families participate. Kids get involved. Customers feel they're earning something valuable. But to run these campaigns smarter, and to deliver measurable value to retailers and brands alike, data and automation must play a bigger role.

At KLR, we’ve embraced this evolution—not by changing the heart of our campaigns, but by making the engine smarter behind the scenes.

Loyalty is about more than stickers. It’s about understanding your customers, delighting them, and using intelligence to build stronger relationships—one sticker at a time.

title: Branding from Within: How Culture, Digital Trends and Courage Shaped KLR
excerpt: Ten years ago, we were nearly invisible. No real online presence, no storytelling—just a logo and some slides. But as the world embraced openness, digital engagement, and human-centered communication, we began a transformation of our own. Slowly, and then all at once, we started
contentHtml:
Overview

Ten years ago, communication in our company looked very different.

We had a logo. We had a very basic website. We had commercial presentations. And that was it. Our presence—both online and offline—was modest, almost silent. We kept things to ourselves, believing that doing good work was enough to speak for us.

But the world changed. And so did we.

As businesses everywhere shifted towards more transparent, digital, and human communication, we found ourselves at a crossroads. We could stay quiet. Or we could take a deep breath and start talking—not just merely about what we offer, but about who we are.

And that made all the difference.

Looking Inward to Look Outward

We’re not an old style top-down company. So when the moment came to redefine how we wanted to show up in the world, we sat together and asked the tough questions:

  • What do we really stand for?
  • What is our brand archetype?
  • What’s our tone of voice—not just in business documents, but in human communications?
  • Are our core values aligned with the perception we want to give?
 

The answers didn’t come from an external agency. They couldn't. 

They came from us—from internal surveys, team discussions, and rounds of feedback. What you see now in our presentations, our website, and on LinkedIn is the result of introspection as much as strategy. Communication, it turns out, wasn’t just about the outside world. It became the mirror in which we saw ourselves more clearly.

From Silence to Strategy: The Post-Pandemic Leap

The pivotal moment came in the years following the pandemic. We made a collective decision: we would start telling our story. Not because it was easy. But because we realised no one else could tell it better.

At the time, we didn’t know how to do it "right." There was no rulebook. No blueprint. Just the belief that if we showed up authentically, we might open doors. And we did—new clients, new countries, new colleagues, new partners. Aligned not just with what we do, but with who we are.

That’s when we saw the true power of corporate communication: not to “show off,” but to show up. Strategically, consistently, and honestly.

How Corporate Communication Has Evolved for All — and What It Means for Us

The last decade has transformed the very role of communication in business. Where once it was a functional tool, today it is a strategic driver—shaping culture, perception, and even growth.

Here’s what’s changed, for the world and for us:

1. From Statements to Conversations

Social media has redefined how we engage. Platforms like LinkedIn give us the ability to connect with stakeholders in real-time, human-to-human. It's no longer just about “content”—it’s about initiating conversation.

2. From Business Card to Dynamic Brand Ecosystems

A website used to be a digital business card just to share your contact details. Now, it’s your brand’s home. Through storytelling, design, and tone, our website finally reflects who we are. It’s a space where our values live and evolve.

3. From Hidden Work to Visible Culture

We used to ask: “Is it really interesting to share this?” But we learned that sharing our processes, our team, our stories—helps people understand us, trust us, and choose us. Visibility builds alignment, both inside and out.

4. From Generic to Personal

Communication today is about relevance. We don’t just speak at people—we speak to them. Whether it's a tailored LinkedIn post, a thoughtful presentation, or a client proposal, we aim to make every message count.

5. From Support Function to Strategic Force

Internally, communication is no longer just about sharing updates. It’s about alignment, culture, and clarity. It helps us grow as a team, connect across countries, and stay inspired—even during busy seasons.

The Struggles Are Real—and Worth It

Our evolution hasn’t been without its challenges:

  • Budget: Let’s be honest—good communication takes resources. It’s not “free” just because it lives on social media or on a website.
  • Time: For communication to feel real, the team needs time to contribute, reflect, and create. We’re still finding that balance.
  • Personal hesitations: Is what I do worth sharing? Does anyone care? What if someone copies us? These are real fears. But with every post, we got braver.
  • Finding the line: Between openness and oversharing. Between humility and visibility. It’s a constant calibration—but we’ve come to trust our instincts.
  • Discipline: Corporate communication isn’t a one-off initiative—it’s an ongoing commitment. Once you start, it becomes part of your rhythm, demanding consistency, care, and continuous effort to keep the message alive and evolving.

What’s Next? More of What Matters

Looking ahead, our goal is to consolidate what we’ve built—and to expand into new, more engaging formats. We want to show not just what we do, but how we think. We want to share more stories, ideas, people, and purpose.

Because communication isn’t just a tool we use—it’s a reflection of who we are. And as a loyalty marketing company, we believe that trust starts with visibility. That relationships are built on stories. That people want to work with people.

And if we can get our story right—maybe we can help others do the same.

So stay tuned.

We’re still evolving. Still creating. Still talking. And we’re just getting started.

title: From the First Steps to Lasting Partnerships: How KLR Europe Built and Evolved Client Relationships
excerpt: Ten years ago, KLR Europe began with a bold vision and a few trusted conversations. With no global portfolio or advanced tools, we built our foundation on passion, trust, and hands-on collaboration. Today, we deliver data-driven loyalty solutions across Europe—but our core values
contentHtml:
Overview

Ten years ago, KLR Europe started with a vision and a handful of conversations. Back then, we didn’t have a global portfolio, big data tools, or decades of case studies to back us up. What we did have was passion, determination, and a commitment to creating loyalty solutions that deliver measurable results.

In those early days, every client meeting felt monumental. Each handshake, each pitch, and each campaign was a milestone. We still remember our first clients—partners who saw potential not just in our services, but in our people. They believed in our ability to understand their needs, act quickly, and deliver value.

The Early Days: Personal Touch, Practical Solutions

Our approach then was simple but powerful:

  • listen deeply
  • act swiftly
  • build trust through action.

We weren’t just solution providers—we were collaborators, often working side-by-side with our clients to tailor campaigns, design engagement strategies, and track results with care.

We learned a lot—about different industries, customer behaviour, and the value of truly understanding what motivates loyalty. Those early campaigns taught us that client relationships aren’t built in boardrooms—they’re built in the trenches.

Evolving with
Our Clients

Fast-forward a decade, and both our team and our technology have grown. Today, KLR delivers loyalty solutions grounded in data, personalisation, and strategic insight. We use powerful tools to analyse behaviour, segment audiences, and design loyalty programs that align with long-term business goals.

But our approach to client relationships? That part hasn’t changed much—at least not at the core. We still believe in being hands-on. We still believe that results matter. And we still prioritise trust above all else.

What has changed is how we deliver. We’ve become more agile, more analytical, and more scalable. Our ability to adapt has allowed us to serve clients across Europe, across industries, and across a rapidly evolving digital landscape.

Over the years, we’ve learned that loyalty isn’t just something we create for our clients—it’s something we live every day. Loyalty to our partners, our team, and our values has shaped the way we grow. It’s why many of our earliest clients are still with us today.
Designer

Loyalty Starts with Us

Loyalty to our partners, our team, and our values has shaped the way we grow. It’s why many of our earliest clients are still with us today.

As we look to the next ten years, we remain committed to evolving with our clients—not just keeping pace with change, but leading with innovation, insight, and integrity.

To all the clients who trusted us from the beginning — and those who’ve joined us along the way — thank you.

Here’s to what we’ve built, and to everything still to come.

title: 10 Years of Evolution: How KLR Transformed Its Data & Management Tools to Scale Success
excerpt: Over the past decade, KLR has evolved from a hands-on startup navigating growing pains to a scalable, data-driven organization that thrives on precision and performance. This transformation wasn’t just about upgrading tools — it was about rethinking how we operate, decide, and gr
contentHtml:
Overview

Ten years ago, KLR was a growing company with a clear mission — but like many businesses on the rise, we faced a critical challenge: how to manage increasing complexity without losing agility or visibility. Fast forward to today, and the transformation we’ve undergone is more than just technical — it’s strategic, cultural, and deeply integrated into how we operate. Here’s a look at the journey we took — and some key lessons we learned along the way.

1. From Spreadsheets to Systems

In the early days, spreadsheets were our lifeline. They were simple, flexible, and — for a time — effective. But as our operations expanded, so did the risk of errors, data silos, and duplicated effort. Our first big shift was moving toward centralized systems that could grow with us.

2. Investing in Data Infrastructure

We recognized early on that good decisions start with good data. That meant moving from reactive reporting to real-time insights. We invested in cloud-based data infrastructure, enabling faster analysis, better forecasting, and smarter resource allocation.

3. Custom Tools for Unique Needs

Off-the-shelf software helped, but it didn’t always fit our workflows. Over time, we developed custom tools that allowed for greater automation, integration, and usability — giving our teams the power to work smarter, not harder.

4. Management by Metrics

With better data came better management. We shifted from gut-based decisions to KPI-driven strategies, aligning goals across departments and ensuring everyone was pulling in the same direction.

5. Cultural Shift Toward Transparency

Technology alone doesn’t transform a company — people do. We cultivated a culture where data is shared, discussed, and used to drive improvement. Transparency has become one of our greatest strengths.

What’s Next?

The journey doesn’t end here. We continue to explore AI, machine learning, and advanced analytics to keep pushing forward.

But one thing is clear: our success over the past 10 years wasn’t just about adopting new tools — it was about building the right mindset to use them.

To those just starting this kind of transformation: be bold, be curious, and build with the future in mind.

Technology alone doesn’t transform a company — people do. We cultivated a culture where data is shared, discussed, and used to drive improvement. Transparency has become one of our greatest strengths.

title: 10 Years of KLR: A Journey Full of People, Emotions, and Growth
excerpt: In just ten years, KLR has grown from three people in a small office to a thriving international team delivering loyalty marketing solutions across 16+ countries. In this personal reflection, CEO Antonio Finazzi shares the story behind the numbers — a journey shaped by ambition,
contentHtml:
Overview

In just ten years, KLR has grown from three people in a small office to a thriving international team delivering loyalty marketing solutions across 20 countries. In this personal reflection, CEO Antonio Finazzi shares the story behind the numbers — a journey shaped by ambition, challenges, mistakes, and, above all, people. This is not just a business milestone. It’s a story of trust, growth, and the power of a united team.

1. From a Small Room to an International Team

10 years ago, we were three people in a tiny office with more ideas than answers. Today, we’re nearly 40, across borders, building something great. 

This is not just a milestone — it’s a story. 

Time — the most precious thing we have and the only one we cannot change, slow down, or speed up. We can only live it and maybe only in retrospect, try to understand it.

10 years of KLR — is it a lot, or not so much? Did it go fast, or did it feel long? 

Actually… both.

When I think about the beginning, the early days, it feels like just a month ago, or maybe a year. I still remember those first car rides to Koper with Stefano, speaking about something we didn’t fully understand yet, imagining goals that still needed to be built…

2. From Three Desks to an International Presence

On the other hand, when I take a step back and look at everything that happened in these 10 years, time suddenly takes on a different dimension: from that small office with 3 desks… to a team of almost 40 people, now working from beautiful offices in Slovenia and Italy and with top clients in more than 16 countries.

We’ve become an international company — and our creativity, professionalism, and service quality are now recognized by the entire loyalty marketing industry.

3. The Power of Firsts

I remember all of our “firsts”: 

the first big meetings with clients… 

the first campaign in Croatia… 

the first time we entered a new country… 

the first day in each new office… 

the first people who joined us…

Then, little by little — and without even realising — the meetings increased, the connections grew stronger, the foundations got deeper. 

At the beginning, success was rare and small, while mistakes were frequent. But step by step, the balance started to change. Growth came naturally.

4. A Decade of Lessons and Emotions

And yes, mistakes are still here — and that’s good because they teach us, they shape us.

All of this… in just 10 years? So many events, so many faces, so many stories and emotions — something that maybe some don’t even experience in 20 or 30 years.

It still feels like just a short time ago, when I was guiding Stefano through the first steps of this exhilarating adventure. Now, here I am, by his side, but this time, it’s him leading the way.

5. Our Greatest Asset: The Team

If this has been a success story, it’s thanks above all to one essential thing: our amazing team. A young and diverse group of people, each coming from completely different journeys…

Different personalities. Different views. Different experiences. But together, we made something truly unique, and, above all, efficient.

Some of them have been with us for many years. Some, since the very beginning, and I’m happy to see how much they’ve grown — not only as professionals, but above all, as persons.

6. The Road Ahead

10 years.

Just the first chapter of a longer road ahead. It won’t be easy. There will be new challenges, new goals, but I know we will face them with strength, competence, professionalism — and unity.

Thank you all for these 10 incredible years, and of course… for the ones still to come.

title: 6 years and not one of them was just a job
excerpt:
In 2025, our Head of Marketing celebrates her 6-year milestone at KLR.
 In her reflective anniversary article, she shares the resilience, creativity, and emotional investment that shaped her role far beyond the job title.
 From problem-solving and cross-functional challenges to i
contentHtml:
Overview

In 2025, our Head of Marketing celebrates her 6-year milestone at KLR.
 In her reflective anniversary article, she shares the resilience, creativity, and emotional investment that shaped her role far beyond the job title.
 From problem-solving and cross-functional challenges to international campaigns and unforgettable teamwork, Marta’s story is one of growth, grit, and gratitude — and a reminder that some careers are built, not given.

Inception

Today’s exactly 6 years since I started my journey at KLR.  In the 6th month of 2019, I became the 6th person in the Rovato office.

There was no marketing roadmap waiting, just square 1 and the quiet challenge of building something that mattered. The structure, logic, and processes we rely on today? They didn’t exist back then. They were built, lived into, sweated out.

And I’ll always be genuinely grateful to the people — you know who you are — who gave me that exact opportunity and trust to take that step then. That trust made me go all in. It turned this from “a job” into something I wanted to shape, protect, and grow, because when something matters to you, you naturally give more of yourself, you feel invested.

Emily NOT in Paris

For quite a while, my name in the company was “marketing”. No one really tells you that building a function often means being the function. 

You don’t just produce outcomes — you:

  • define the logic
  • shape the direction
  • give the work meaning. 

I was the planner, the manager, the copywriter, the analyst, the researcher, the problem solver, the art director, you name it — often all on the same day.

Today, I manage a team, yes. But here’s what many don’t see: half of them joined just over a year ago. 

Late nights? Yes. 

Moments that felt heavy? Also yes. 

It takes resilience to show up when the results live in a hypothetical future and the effort is silent. 

And I am grateful for those moments!  

Mad Men

And there’s something powerful about seeing your work live — in stores, across borders, in real life.  

The first one I remember? Two months in, a radio ad in a language I did not completely understand but I’d written the script for. 

Then different markets, different rules. Same core truth: 

Creativity starts when the comfort zone ends. Success starts where the comfort zone ends. Growth starts where the comfort zone ends.

Each campaign launched was a reminder that this was never just a job. It was creation. Connection. Challenge. Great teamwork. And I was part of it. Wonderful. 

Casino Royale

And when I rolled the dice 6 years ago…I hit 6 points. 🎲 And then came the series of moments no one could’ve scripted. Not everything was built through structure and process. Some things were luck. Some were timing. High-stakes days. A little chaos. A lot of magic.

  • Meeting the most incredible people. Some projects pass. People stay.
  • Creating the impossible — and then doing it again.
  • Seeing the result of months of work — live, in stores, across countries. The adrenaline? Unmatched.
  • Traveling for work. Every flight, every meeting — expanding my world.
  • Building memories that still make me smile in the most random moments.

In the next episodes

To everyone I’ve crossed paths with along the way — thank you!

 For the support, the advice, the challenges, the example. For the healthy pressure, the shared wins, the quiet help when it was needed most. For every chat, every brainstorm, every disagreement that pushed things forward. You’ve shaped this ride more than you know.

 And I’m grateful I got to grow through it — with you, because of you, and sometimes in spite of you.

For me, this place has been a playground for ideas, a pressure cooker for resilience, and a stage for impact. Here’s to the journey so far — and whatever’s next. 

That’s all, folks! (for now…)

title: Strategic Loyalty Marketing Insights from Loyalty Connect Global 2025 - Dubai
excerpt:
At Dubai Loyalty Connect 2025, industry leaders made it clear: loyalty is no longer a side strategy—it’s central to growth. For supermarkets and petrol stations, this means rethinking programs as engines of behavior change, emotional connection, and business intelligence.
From fa
contentHtml:
Overview

As loyalty professionals gathered in Dubai for Loyalty Connect 2025, one message rang loud and clear: loyalty is no longer just a marketing tool—it’s the engine of growth, data strategy, and brand love.

For KLR, with our deep roots in the petrol and supermarket sectors across Europe, these insights reaffirm our approach: loyalty must be human-centric, data-informed, and business-driven. Here's a deeper look at the trends shaping the future—and what they mean for our clients.

From Points to Purpose: Strategic Loyalty Insights from Dubai Loyalty Connect 2025

1. Loyalty Is Now a Business Essential,
Not a Bonus

Gone are the days when loyalty programs were seen as “nice to have.” Today, they are core to competitive strategy. As highlighted in multiple keynotes, loyalty is the new battleground—not only for customer attention but also for revenue.

💡 Implication for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

  • Loyalty must be tied to core business metrics:
    • basket size
    • visit frequency
    • fuel volume
    • share of wallet.

That means programs must integrate seamlessly with retail operations, influencing not only customer behavior but also inventory, pricing, and promotions.

  • Investments in loyalty should be justified not just by retention, but by measurable, incremental gains—in sales, data capture, and long-term customer value.
“Loyalty marketing is about driving profitable behaviour change.”
Paula Thomas
Designer

2. Redefining Loyalty: It's About Profitable Behaviour Change

As Paula Thomas put it: “Loyalty marketing is about driving profitable behaviour change.”

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

Encourage specific behaviours aligned with business goals:

  • Shopping midweek
  • Choosing bundled or high-margin services
  • Using in-app ordering or digital fuel payments

This is where loyalty becomes a tool of strategic influence—not just retention.

3. Speed to First Redemption: A Loyalty Growth Lever

One of the most powerful insights came from American Airlines: “Burn drives earn.” Customers must feel value early in their journey.

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

Design seamless early-win mechanics:

  • First-fill discounts at the pump
  • Introductory grocery coupons
  • Tier upgrades within 2 visits
  • Early redemptions drive emotional stickiness and program momentum.

4. Strategic Partnerships: Loyalty’s Competitive Multiplier

From Royal Bank of Canada’s 2,000+ brand alliance to Maxxing’s emphasis on emotional relevance, one idea resonated across the Dubai stage: partnerships are the infrastructure of modern loyalty.

No single brand can meet all customer needs alone. Strategic collaborations enable programs to expand value, reach new audiences, and stay culturally relevant—without diluting their brand identity.

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

For supermarkets and petrol stations, smart partnerships could mean:

  • Cross-brand reward ecosystems (fuel + grocery + delivery)
  • Lifestyle integrations (e.g. wellness, streaming, transport)
  • Purpose-based alliances (e.g. sustainability rewards, donation redemptions)

Done right, partnerships do more than share costs—they:

  • Enrich customer experiences
  • Multiply engagement opportunities
  • Monetize loyalty ecosystems as B2B media platforms
  • Reinforce brand purpose and social alignment

5. Loyalty as a Media Business

Brands like Walgreens and United Airlines are monetising their loyalty ecosystems—turning apps and data into ad delivery and media platforms.

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

Your loyalty program is a media channel. Sponsored offers, retail media banners, and branded gamification can create new B2B revenue streams, especially in high-footfall environments like supermarkets and petrol stations.

6. Emotions Matter: Loyalty Beyond Transactions

Many speakers stressed that loyalty today is driven as much by emotion as by value.

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

  • Surprise customers with unexpected perks
  • Let them donate points to causes
  • Use scent, nostalgia, and recognition to create memorable experiences

Emotionally resonant programs outperform transactional ones, especially with younger generations.

7. The Loyalty Boom:
Be Ready for Rapid Growth

The loyalty industry is forecast to triple to $41 billion by 2032 (Statista). This is a growth moment—and a competitive one.

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

Future-proof your programs with:

  • Modular design
  • AI-powered personalization
  • Seamless omnichannel integration
  • Data-sharing architecture for partnerships

8. Measuring What Matters: KPIs that Drive Loyalty Success

The most successful programs measure what matters—not just enrollment, but behaviour change and ROI.

  • Speed to first redemption – the shorter the time to the first reward, the stronger the long-term engagement
  • Cost-to-retain vs. cost-to-acquire – proving loyalty as a more sustainable growth lever than discount-driven acquisition
 

💡 Implications for Supermarkets & Petrol Stations:

Loyalty marketing campaigns should track KPIs aligned to both commercial and emotional loyalty:

  • Incremental revenue per member
  • Visit frequency uplift
  • Share-of-wallet gain
  • Redemption velocity and satisfaction
  • Net Promoter Score (NPS) movement among members
  • Partner program ROI (where applicable)

By focusing on these indicators, loyalty becomes measurable, optimisable—and ultimately more investable at board level.

Final Thought:
Loyalty That Goes Beyond Points

At KLR, we’ve always believed that true loyalty is earned—not bought.

It comes from understanding the customer journey, offering real value, and delivering experiences that make people come back—not because they have to, but because they want to.

The insights from Dubai reinforce this vision—and we’re more energized than ever to help our clients shape the future of loyalty in supermarkets and fuel retail.

title: 10 Years of Growth: A Journey Shaped by People
excerpt: KLR was never just a business idea. It started with a friendship — and a shared belief that people, not processes, are what make the difference. As we celebrate 10 years, we’re proud of the culture we’ve built: one rooted in curiosity, courage, and a genuine desire to grow — toge
contentHtml:
Overview

KLR was never just a business idea. It started with a friendship — and a shared belief that people, not processes, are what make the difference. As we celebrate 10 years, we’re proud of the culture we’ve built: one rooted in curiosity, courage, and a genuine desire to grow — together. This is the story of how we turned a simple idea into a thriving company, one bold step at a time.

This year marks a big milestone for us at KLR: 10 years since we started this adventure. It’s the perfect moment to pause, take a breath, and reflect on how far we’ve come — and, more importantly, why we’ve come this far.

KLR was born from something simple and powerful: A friendship between people from very different cultures and backgrounds, and an idea about doing business differently — with people at the centre.

From the very beginning, that mix of diversity, curiosity, and mutual respect shaped who we are. We didn’t set out with a grand business plan. We started with a belief: if we bring the right people together — people who care, who listen, who are open to learning — we can build something special. And we did.

The Power of Curiosity
(and a Little Bit of Courage)

We quickly understood that experience on paper wasn’t going to be the key to our success. The world changes fast — technologies, markets, people’s needs — and you can’t face that with rigid expertise alone. What we needed was something different: curiosity, flexibility, the courage to jump in and figure things out along the way.

Our work is highly specific — there’s no university degree in “loyalty solutions,” no ready-made experts waiting to be hired. So instead of chasing the impossible, we focused on what was right in front of us: people with potential, with energy, with a hunger to grow.

We built a culture around “learning by doing” — because real growth happens on the job, in real projects, with real clients. That mindset didn’t just help us evolve internally — it became part of how we work with our clients too. We grow together, side by side, adapting and improving with every step.

Growing a Team, Growing a Culture

When we brought people into the KLR family, we looked for something very specific: a willingness to learn. 

That’s it. 

More than any title, diploma or past role, we valued the mindset — that inner drive to keep pushing forward, to ask questions, to try, fail, learn, and try again.

What we’ve built together isn’t just a team — it’s a culture. A place where people feel safe to share ideas, to challenge each other, to be themselves. We’ve created an environment where everyone can take ownership of their path, contribute their unique strengths, and support each other along the way.

We’re not a “copy-paste” company. And honestly, we’re proud of that. We’ve never wanted to follow a standard template. We want to keep creating, experimenting, listening. That’s how we’ve managed to deliver smart, impactful loyalty solutions — not by repeating what others do, but by designing what each client really needs.

What Comes Next

Looking back, what moves me most is the community we’ve built. Not just inside KLR, but with our clients and partners across Europe. So many relationships built on trust, transparency, and shared ambition. These are more than business connections — they’re real partnerships, and in many cases, real friendships.

As we celebrate this anniversary, I feel incredibly grateful. To the team — past and present — who’ve given their best, who’ve grown with us, who’ve made KLR what it is. To our clients, who’ve believed in us, challenged us, and grown with us too. To everyone who’s been part of the ride — thank you.

Here’s to 10 amazing years. And here’s to the future — to new challenges, new ideas, and many more shared successes. We’re just getting started.

title: The importance of time off: 6 secrets of work-life balance
excerpt: What to say that was not already said on this topic? I can share the importance of time off looking at my personal experience and how it works for me. We work to live, right? We would be doing something else if it were not for the circumstances that lead us to do what we do. It i
contentHtml:
Overview

What to say that was not already said on this topic? I can share the importance of time off looking at my personal experience and how it works for me. We work to live, right? We would be doing something else if it were not for the circumstances that lead us to do what we do. It is unavoidable and therefore needs to be balanced well in order not to cause more bad than good, apart from the money earned.

1. The Uniqueness of Work-Life Balance

For sure there is no simple formula that can work for everyone, as we are all different, but I can say that a work-life balance is particularly important for long-term efficiency and productivity. Not taking time off will inevitably lead to complications with our physical or mental health that will at some point make it hard for us to perform our tasks or make others not like to work with us. We can push hard for some time, being motivated by reaching our goals, but this cannot endure forever.

2. The Crucial Role of Balance

Like for everything in life, the balance in work-life balance is crucial. In Europe, even more in Slovenia where KLR has its HQ, employees are already very well protected by regulations. Limiting the number of working hours/days, mandating paid time off, and necessitating employers to stimulate a healthy relationship of employees with their work.

3. Personal Determination and Health

But still, our own determination can be our biggest enemy. Soon it will be 10 years of my work with KLR and lately, also due to being in my mid-forties, I found myself being stopped from being able to work, by my body. Getting ill or being hit by acute back pain that made it impossible to stand for some time. This was very much due to work-related fatigue and a consequence of the kind of work that we do, that requires us to sit long hours at our desk where our brains work hard, but our body does nothing at all, if not being put in awkward and unnatural positions for prolonged periods of time.

4. The Missteps in Sporadic Exercise

Trying to do sport sporadically did not make the situation any better. Probably worsened, as an untrained body suffered the determination of the mind to try to recover quickly all undone trainings, adventures, and so on. This even worsened the body situation.

5. Quality Over Quantity in Work-Life Balance

Therefore, for a successful work-life balance, at least in my case, it is not so much about dedicating the right amount of time to work and to life, but rather the quality of the time spent at work and at life. Always we need to feel good at work and at home; if we do not, we are going in the wrong direction and there is no excuse for this.

6. Building a Sustainable Routine

So now I will try to rebuild a new sustainable life routine that can work till I will not change again and will require new adjustments. I will take breaks often, stay hydrated (as five coffees a day are not hydration), stretch and do movement in the office, move my body as we are built to move. And hopefully, I will be able to work forever as work will be no pain, just gain.

The Key to Long-Term Success

TLDR: My recipe for a successful work-life balance is to strive to feel good, both at work and in life. If you are feeling bad you are doing it wrong. Being able to feel good often is the key to a long-term and successful relationship with our work and having a good life where we gain, without too much pain 😉.

title: Growing Together: Our 2024 Team-Building Experience in Franciacorta

Whether in big leaps or small steps, the growth of our people is what makes KLR special. Seeing our team flourish—personally and professionally—is one of the most rewarding parts of leadership.

title: 10 Loyalty Insights from Milano Home
excerpt: The loyalty marketing landscape is rapidly evolving, especially in the supermarket sector. Attending the conference BRAND POWER at MILANO HOME Exhibition provided valuable insights into current trends, innovative strategies, and practical case studies. Here's a comprehensive over
contentHtml:
Overview

The loyalty marketing landscape is rapidly evolving, especially in the supermarket sector. Attending the conference BRAND POWER at MILANO HOME Exhibition provided valuable insights into current trends, innovative strategies, and practical case studies. Here's a comprehensive overview of the key takeaways that my colleague Marta Marga and me summed up:

1. Targeting the Right Audience

A pivotal theme was the importance of focusing on the best customers rather than attempting to satisfy everyone. Loyalty programs should be designed to identify and reward the most valuable patrons. For example, Walmart's approach with its partnership with Yellowstone shows that aligning with popular American culture can effectively attract top-tier customers without diluting brand integrity.

2. Innovative Loyalty Program Structures

  1. Exclusive Membership Clubs: Creating a sense of exclusivity by offering non-monetary benefits can foster deeper customer loyalty. American Express was highlighted for its tiered, distinctive system that goes beyond monetary rewards, emphasizing status and unique experiences.

  2. Retail Card & External Subscription Pairing: Pairing in-store retail cards with external subscriptions adds value and convenience, enhancing customer stickiness.

  3. Utility Rewards: Moving beyond traditional spend-based rewards, innovative programs now reward specific customer behaviors, promoting actions aligned with brand values.

3. Localizing Loyalty Campaigns

LIDL’s collaboration with Emily in Paris demonstrated the power of leveraging local culture and aspirational lifestyles in marketing. The idea of "traveling through products" taps into the consumer's desire for exploration and discovery

4. Seasonal and Behavioral Incentives

  1. Timed Rewards: Providing extra points on traditionally low-traffic days encourages visits during these periods, balancing store footfall.

  2. End-of-Month Spending Incentives: Programs like end-of-month spending rewards have shown to significantly increase redemption rates.

5. Storytelling Elements

Storytelling plays a crucial role in loyalty marketing:

  • Storytelling in rewards makes the experience memorable. The physical rewards should embody a story, transforming them from mere objects into cherished mementos.

6. Data-Driven Strategies

The effective use of CRM and data analytics is central to modern loyalty programs. By understanding and predicting customer behaviors, supermarkets can:

  • Reward loyal customers more effectively.

  • Encourage less frequent shoppers to increase their engagement.

  • Personalize offers that resonate emotionally and practically.

7. The Role of Emotional Marketing

Emotional marketing was emphasized as a tool to convert users into loyal customers. By tapping into fundamental human emotions, brands can create stronger connections. The analogy of tasting the same wine in different glasses illustrates how presentation (the ‘how’) is as critical as the product itself (the ‘what’).

8. Social Impact Initiatives

Campaigns with social causes, such as supporting women victims of violence or health-related charities, were discussed as powerful loyalty tools. These initiatives not only promote customer engagement but also enhance brand reputation and loyalty.

9. Moving Beyond Price-Based Promotions

The conference highlighted the declining effectiveness of manipulative marketing tactics focused solely on pricing. Customers now seek genuine value and authenticity, pressing brands to adopt a more holistic approach to loyalty.

10. Brand Loyalty and Consumer Faithfulness

An intriguing perspective was presented: it is the brand that needs to be loyal to the consumer, not just the other way around. By consistently delivering on promises and exceeding expectations, brands can cultivate a loyal customer base while creating disloyalty towards competitors.

Conclusion

The loyalty marketing landscape is becoming increasingly sophisticated, blending emotional engagement, strategic incentives, and data-driven personalization. Supermarkets are uniquely positioned to leverage these trends to build robust, long-lasting relationships with their customers. As the industry evolves, staying adaptable and customer-centric will be the key to success.
